summary: The app writes the exact Microsoft-documented NTLMMinClientSec combination for NTLM SSP based clients: NTLMv2 session security plus 128-bit encryption. The tweak is now modeled with the official NTLM SSP client scope instead of the previous SMB-only wording.

setting.name: Require NTLM SSP Client Session Security and 128-bit Encryption
setting.casual_explanation: This setting raises the minimum session security requirements for NTLM SSP based clients. In the app's enabled state it requires both NTLMv2 session security and 128-bit encryption.
setting.why_people_change_it: People enable it when they want stronger legacy authentication protections for NTLM-based client sessions. They leave it lower when they still need compatibility with older systems or software that cannot meet those requirements.
setting.tradeoffs_overview: Requiring both NTLMv2 session security and 128-bit encryption hardens NTLM SSP based client sessions, but it can reduce compatibility with older servers, devices, or software that only support weaker NTLM negotiation.
setting.professional_notes: Microsoft documents NTLMMinClientSec under HKLM\System\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa\MSV1_0 as the client-side NTLM SSP session security option. The Policy CSP and the local sceregvl metadata expose the same flag-style values: 524288 for NTLMv2 session security, 536870912 for 128-bit encryption, and 537395200 for both together. The app writes the exact documented 537395200 combination and now labels it with the correct NTLM SSP client scope.

allowed_values.label: Windows managed baseline
allowed_values.meaning: Windows keeps its default NTLM SSP client security posture when no explicit override is present.

allowed_values.label: No extra minimum flags
allowed_values.meaning: No additional NTLM session security minimum is forced through this value.

allowed_values.label: Require NTLMv2 session security
allowed_values.meaning: Require NTLMv2 session security for NTLM SSP based clients.

allowed_values.label: Require 128-bit encryption
allowed_values.meaning: Require 128-bit encryption for NTLM SSP based clients.

allowed_values.label: Require NTLMv2 session security and 128-bit encryption
allowed_values.meaning: Require both NTLMv2 session security and 128-bit encryption for NTLM SSP based clients.

app_current_implementation.notes: The app writes the exact Microsoft-documented NTLM SSP client security option: NTLMMinClientSec = 537395200 under HKLM\System\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa\MSV1_0. The UX now reflects the documented NTLM SSP client scope instead of an SMB-only description.

windows_defaults.label: Windows security-option default
windows_defaults.applies_to: Windows systems exposing the NTLM SSP client security option

states.rationale: The policy can be left unset so Windows keeps the built-in baseline.

windows_defaults.notes: This record documents the Microsoft NTLM SSP client security option directly.

recommended_profiles.label: Windows managed default
intended_for: Systems that should keep the normal Windows baseline
avoid_for: Environments that explicitly need stronger NTLM client requirements

states.rationale: Leave the explicit security-option override absent.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You want Windows to keep the normal NTLM SSP client baseline.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You need an explicitly hardened NTLM SSP client minimum.
tradeoffs: No explicit NTLM SSP client hardening flags are forced.
Compatibility remains at the default Windows baseline.

recommended_profiles.label: Require NTLM SSP client session security and 128-bit encryption
intended_for: Environments that still use NTLM and want stronger client-side session protection
Machines that must enforce both NTLMv2 session security and 128-bit encryption
avoid_for: Legacy systems or software that cannot negotiate the stronger NTLM SSP requirements

states.rationale: Require both NTLMv2 session security and 128-bit encryption for NTLM SSP based clients.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You need the strongest documented NTLM SSP client minimum supported by this control.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You still depend on older NTLM peers that cannot satisfy NTLMv2 session security or 128-bit encryption.
tradeoffs: The exact Microsoft-documented NTLM SSP client hardening flags are applied.
Older peers that cannot meet the stronger NTLM SSP requirements may fail.
recommended_profiles.notes: Published control aligned to the official Microsoft NTLM SSP client security option.

evidence.title: Microsoft Policy CSP: LocalPoliciesSecurityOptions / NTLM SSP based clients
evidence.summary: Microsoft documents the official NTLMMinClientSec path and the supported numeric values 0, 524288, 536870912, and 537395200 for NTLM SSP based clients.

evidence.title: Local Windows sceregvl NTLMMinClientSec metadata
evidence.summary: The local security-option metadata maps NTLMMinClientSec and exposes the two flags used for NTLM session security and 128-bit encryption.

evidence.title: Current app implementation
evidence.summary: The provider writes NTLMMinClientSec = 537395200 and labels the tweak as requiring NTLM SSP client session security and 128-bit encryption.

decision.why: The app writes the exact Microsoft-documented NTLM SSP client security value combination and now presents it with the official scope instead of the earlier SMB-only wording.

validation_proof.exact_quote_or_path: Network security: Minimum session security for NTLM SSP based (including secure RPC) clients ... Allowed values: 0 None. 524288 Require NTLMv2 session security. 536870912 (Default) Require 128-bit encryption. 537395200 Require NTLM and 128-bit encryption.
validation_proof.notes: Microsoft directly documents NTLMMinClientSec for NTLM SSP based clients and the exact 537395200 value combination. Local sceregvl metadata independently exposes the same NTLMv2 session security and 128-bit encryption bit flags.
